sections.plain_summary: Fibromyalgia is a chronic condition characterised by widespread musculoskeletal pain, profound fatigue, and a heightened sensitivity to pressure and touch throughout the body. It is now understood as a disorder of pain processing in the central nervous system, where pain signals are amplified beyond what would normally be expected from any tissue injury. There is no structural damage to muscles or joints — the nervous system itself becomes dysregulated, producing real pain without a peripheral source. It frequently occurs alongside other conditions such as IBS, sleep disorders, anxiety, and chronic fatigue.
sections.detailed_summary:
Fibromyalgia is one of the most common chronic pain conditions, affecting an estimated 2–4% of the global population. Despite decades of controversy, it is now well-supported by neuroimaging and neurochemical research: people with fibromyalgia show objectively different patterns of brain activation in response to painful stimuli, elevated levels of pain-sensitising neurotransmitters such as substance P and glutamate, and reduced levels of inhibitory signals such as serotonin and norepinephrine. This is often described as central sensitisation — a state in which the central nervous system becomes persistently over-responsive, amplifying all sensory input.

The condition typically develops after a triggering event — a physical illness, injury, surgery, significant psychological stress, or trauma — although in some individuals onset is gradual without a clear precipitant. Women are diagnosed significantly more often than men, though male cases are thought to be systematically underdiagnosed. There is no diagnostic blood test or imaging finding; diagnosis is made clinically based on symptom pattern and the exclusion of other conditions.

Core symptoms extend well beyond pain: cognitive dysfunction ('fibro fog'), non-restorative sleep, fatigue, headaches, and mood disturbance are integral features rather than secondary complaints. Gastrointestinal symptoms are extremely common and many individuals carry a concurrent IBS diagnosis. Sensory hypersensitivity — to light, sound, smell, and temperature — is frequently reported and fits within the central sensitisation framework.

Management is most effective when it is multimodal, combining graded movement, sleep improvement, pain neuroscience education, psychological approaches such as CBT or ACT, and in some cases medication. No single treatment resolves fibromyalgia, and many individuals cycle through disappointing medication trials before finding a broader approach that provides meaningful improvement. Self-directed investigation and research are common and legitimate — many people arrive at useful frameworks for their condition before any clinician does.

prevalence.summary: Affects roughly 2–4% of the global population, making it one of the most common chronic pain conditions.
prevalence.estimate: 2–4% of the general population; some estimates range up to 7% depending on diagnostic criteria used
prevalence.notes: Women are diagnosed 2–3 times more often than men, though this likely reflects both biological predisposition and diagnostic bias — fibromyalgia in men is frequently missed or attributed to other causes. Prevalence rises with age, peaking in the 40–60 age group. The condition is likely underdiagnosed globally, with average diagnostic delay of several years in many countries.

subgroups.name: Post-infectious fibromyalgia
subgroups.description: Onset following a documented infectious illness — viral, bacterial, or parasitic. Conceptually overlaps with post-COVID syndrome and post-Lyme presentations. Central sensitisation may be triggered or accelerated by the immune and neurological effects of infection.
distinguishing_features: Clear temporal link between infection and symptom onset
May have additional features of post-viral illness such as post-exertional malaise
Often presents alongside fatigue severe enough to overlap with ME/CFS criteria
May respond differently to exercise-based approaches — pushing through fatigue can worsen symptoms

subgroups.name: Trauma-associated fibromyalgia
subgroups.description: Develops following physical or psychological trauma, including accidents, surgery, adverse childhood experiences, or PTSD. The nervous system dysregulation in trauma and in fibromyalgia share overlapping mechanisms.
distinguishing_features: Strong comorbidity with PTSD, complex trauma, or childhood adversity
Autonomic nervous system dysregulation may be prominent (hypervigilance, sleep disruption, startle responses)
Psychological trauma history is relevant to treatment planning
Trauma-informed approaches to pain management are particularly important in this group

subgroups.name: Fibromyalgia with prominent cognitive symptoms
subgroups.description: A subgroup in which cognitive dysfunction — brain fog, memory problems, word-finding difficulty — is as disabling as the pain itself. Sometimes called 'fibro fog'.
distinguishing_features: Memory problems, difficulty concentrating, and slowed thinking are foregrounded
Cognitive symptoms often fluctuate with pain flares
Can overlap with ADHD presentations, making differential diagnosis complex
More likely to interfere significantly with work and daily functioning

subgroups.name: Fibromyalgia overlapping with hypermobility (hEDS/HSD)
subgroups.description: A significant proportion of people with hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome or hypermobility spectrum disorder also meet fibromyalgia criteria. The joint instability and repeated microtrauma in hypermobility may contribute to or amplify central sensitisation.
distinguishing_features: Joint hypermobility on examination (Beighton score)
History of frequent sprains, dislocations, or joint pain since childhood
May have additional features of connective tissue fragility: soft skin, easy bruising, fatigue
Standard fibromyalgia exercise recommendations may need modification to protect unstable joints

subgroups.name: Fibromyalgia with autonomic dysfunction (POTS overlap)
subgroups.description: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) and fibromyalgia co-occur at higher-than-expected rates, possibly because both involve dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system.
distinguishing_features: Lightheadedness, palpitations, or near-fainting on standing
Fatigue is disproportionately severe
Heart rate increases significantly on moving from lying to standing
Temperature dysregulation and excessive sweating may be present

symptoms.symptom: Widespread musculoskeletal pain affecting multiple body regions simultaneously
symptoms.notes: By definition must be present in multiple areas of the body. Often described as aching, burning, or stabbing. Can shift location day to day.

symptoms.symptom: Allodynia — pain from normally non-painful stimuli such as light touch or clothing
symptoms.notes: A hallmark of central sensitisation. Even gentle pressure or texture on skin can be acutely painful, especially during flares.

symptoms.symptom: Tender points — localised areas of exaggerated pain on pressure
symptoms.notes: Previously central to diagnosis (18 tender point criteria), now less emphasised but still clinically recognised. Found at characteristic sites including neck, shoulders, hips, knees.

symptoms.symptom: Profound, persistent fatigue not relieved by sleep
symptoms.notes: Distinct from ordinary tiredness. Many people describe feeling exhausted regardless of sleep duration. Fatigue often rivals pain as the most disabling feature.

symptoms.symptom: Non-restorative sleep — waking unrefreshed despite adequate hours in bed
symptoms.notes: Studies show disruption of slow-wave (deep) sleep in fibromyalgia. Alpha wave intrusion during NREM sleep is a documented finding. Poor sleep worsens pain the next day, creating a reinforcing cycle.

symptoms.symptom: Cognitive dysfunction ('fibro fog') — difficulty with memory, concentration, and word-finding
symptoms.notes: Reported by the majority of people with fibromyalgia. Neuroimaging shows objectively different patterns of cognitive processing. Often fluctuates with pain intensity and sleep quality.
Occur in a significant proportion of people with fibromyalgia. Likely share a common mechanism of central sensitisation and altered pain modulation.

symptoms.symptom: Hypersensitivity to sensory stimuli — light, sound, smell, and temperature
symptoms.notes: Often described as 'sensory overload'. Consistent with a generally dysregulated nervous system amplifying all incoming signals, not just pain.

symptoms.symptom: Irritable bowel symptoms — abdominal pain, bloating, altered bowel habits
symptoms.notes: IBS is diagnosed in an estimated 30–70% of people with fibromyalgia. Both conditions involve central sensitisation of the gut-brain axis. The gut pain in IBS may share the same amplification mechanism as somatic pain.

symptoms.symptom: Morning stiffness lasting more than 15–30 minutes
symptoms.notes: Unlike rheumatoid arthritis, stiffness in fibromyalgia is not associated with joint inflammation or elevated inflammatory markers. Duration is usually shorter than in RA.

symptoms.symptom: Mood disturbance — anxiety, low mood, or emotional dysregulation
symptoms.notes: Bidirectional relationship: mood disorders share neurochemical pathways with fibromyalgia (serotonin, norepinephrine). It is often unclear which came first, and both require attention.

symptoms.symptom: Restless legs syndrome — uncomfortable urge to move legs, especially at night
symptoms.notes: Significantly more prevalent in people with fibromyalgia than in the general population. Contributes to sleep disruption and fatigue.

symptoms.symptom: Bladder urgency or pain (interstitial cystitis-like symptoms)
symptoms.notes: Bladder hypersensitivity without infection or structural cause is common. Considered another manifestation of widespread central sensitisation.

symptoms.symptom: Jaw pain and temporomandibular joint (TMJ) symptoms
symptoms.notes: TMJ disorder is disproportionately common in fibromyalgia. Facial pain, jaw clicking, and difficulty chewing may occur.

symptoms.symptom: Post-exertional symptom flares following physical or cognitive activity
symptoms.notes: Many people experience a worsening of pain and fatigue 12–48 hours after exertion. This is sometimes called post-exertional malaise, though this term is more formally associated with ME/CFS.

symptoms.symptom: Raynaud's phenomenon — colour changes in fingers or toes in response to cold or stress
symptoms.notes: Occurs more frequently in fibromyalgia than in the general population. May reflect autonomic nervous system dysregulation.

symptoms.symptom: Dry eyes and dry mouth (sicca symptoms)
symptoms.notes: Reported by a subset of people with fibromyalgia. Overlaps with Sjögren's syndrome, which must be ruled out by a rheumatologist.

key_patterns: Widespread pain (present in multiple body quadrants for at least 3 months) with no other cause identified after workup
Normal or near-normal standard laboratory results — absence of inflammation markers, normal ANA, normal thyroid, normal full blood count — in the context of significant symptoms
Widespread Pain Index (WPI) ≥ 7 combined with Symptom Severity Scale (SSS) ≥ 5, or WPI 4–6 with SSS ≥ 9 (2016 ACR diagnostic criteria)
Pattern of non-restorative sleep, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms accompanying widespread pain
History of a triggering event (illness, trauma, surgery, stress) followed by onset or marked worsening of pain
Disproportionate pain responses to examination — pain on pressure at multiple sites with normal joint and tissue findings

at_home.name: Symptom diary with pain mapping
at_home.description: Keeping a daily log of pain locations, intensity (0–10 scale), sleep quality, fatigue, and potential triggers helps establish the fluctuating, widespread pattern characteristic of fibromyalgia. A body diagram to mark pain locations each day can reveal the multi-site, shifting nature of the pain and distinguish it from localised structural problems.
at_home.safety_notes: This is a safe observational practice. Obsessive tracking can increase pain focus in some people — aim for a brief daily record rather than continuous monitoring.

at_home.name: Sleep quality self-assessment
at_home.description: Tracking how rested one feels on waking (regardless of hours slept) is a key data point. Non-restorative sleep is a central feature of fibromyalgia and useful to document for clinical appointments. Note whether poor sleep reliably worsens pain the following day.
at_home.safety_notes: No safety concerns. Some sleep tracking devices (wearables) can increase anxiety about sleep quality — use them as a data tool rather than a performance measure.

at_home.name: Activity-symptom pacing log
at_home.description: Recording what activities precede flares helps identify post-exertional patterns. Note the timing: fibromyalgia flares often occur 12–48 hours after an activity, rather than immediately. This information shapes safe activity management and is useful evidence for clinicians.
at_home.safety_notes: Avoid using this log to justify complete inactivity — gradual movement remains important. The goal is pattern recognition, not avoidance.

at_home.name: 2016 ACR Self-Report Criteria (online tools)
at_home.description: The 2016 American College of Rheumatology criteria include a patient-reported Widespread Pain Index and Symptom Severity Scale that can be completed independently. Several validated online versions exist. These do not replace clinical diagnosis but can help individuals recognise whether their symptom pattern fits and approach a clinician with structured information.
at_home.safety_notes: Self-scoring tools are a starting point only. Many other conditions can produce similar symptom patterns and should be ruled out clinically.

clinical.name: Exclusion blood panel
clinical.description: There is no diagnostic blood test for fibromyalgia. Clinical evaluation requires ruling out conditions that mimic it: full blood count, ESR, CRP, ANA, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, thyroid function (TSH/T4), vitamin D, B12, ferritin, calcium, and fasting glucose. In fibromyalgia these are typically normal or only mildly abnormal, which is itself diagnostically informative.
clinical.availability: Orderable by any GP or general practitioner. A rheumatologist will often order a more comprehensive panel.

clinical.name: Clinical examination using 2016 ACR Diagnostic Criteria
clinical.description: The current gold standard is a clinical assessment applying the 2016 American College of Rheumatology criteria: Widespread Pain Index (WPI) scoring 19 anatomical regions, and Symptom Severity Scale (SSS) covering fatigue, non-restorative sleep, and cognitive symptoms. No tender point count is required under current criteria, though palpation of characteristic tender sites remains informative.
clinical.availability: Should be performed by a rheumatologist, pain specialist, or experienced GP. Many clinicians remain unfamiliar with current criteria and still apply outdated criteria — bringing a printed copy of the 2016 criteria to appointments can be helpful.

clinical.name: Polysomnography (sleep study)
clinical.description: Formal overnight sleep study measuring brain activity, breathing, oxygen levels, and movement. Can identify alpha-wave intrusion during deep sleep (associated with fibromyalgia), sleep apnoea, or periodic limb movement disorder — all of which worsen fibromyalgia symptoms if present and untreated.
clinical.availability: Referral through a GP or sleep medicine specialist. Not routinely ordered for fibromyalgia but indicated when sleep apnoea or significant sleep disorder is suspected.

clinical.name: Quantitative Sensory Testing (QST)
clinical.description: Standardised protocol measuring pain thresholds and sensory processing using calibrated pressure, temperature, and vibrational stimuli. Can objectively document central sensitisation and abnormal pain modulation. Used in research settings and increasingly in specialised pain clinics.
clinical.availability: Available at academic pain centres and some specialist clinics. Not routinely available in general practice. Useful in complex or contested cases.

clinical.name: Functional MRI (fMRI) — research context
clinical.description: Research studies have consistently demonstrated abnormal patterns of brain activation in people with fibromyalgia, including altered default mode network connectivity and amplified responses to pain stimuli. Not a clinical diagnostic tool but provides strong mechanistic validation that fibromyalgia involves measurable neurological changes.
clinical.availability: Research settings only. Not available as a clinical diagnostic test.

clinical.name: Rheumatological assessment
clinical.description: Full joint and musculoskeletal examination to rule out inflammatory arthritis, lupus, Sjögren's syndrome, myositis, and other systemic rheumatological conditions that can mimic or co-exist with fibromyalgia.
clinical.availability: Via referral to a rheumatologist. Often the specialist who makes or confirms the fibromyalgia diagnosis after excluding other conditions.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Central sensitisation is the core neurological mechanism underlying fibromyalgia — the persistent up-regulation of pain processing in the central nervous system that produces amplified, widespread pain without peripheral tissue damage. Fibromyalgia is often considered the most common clinical expression of central sensitisation syndrome.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: IBS is present in an estimated 30–70% of people with fibromyalgia, far exceeding chance. Both conditions involve central sensitisation of visceral and somatic pain pathways and share features of gut-brain axis dysregulation. They frequently co-occur as part of a broader central sensitivity syndrome.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome and fibromyalgia co-occur at significantly elevated rates. Both involve autonomic nervous system dysregulation, and the overlap suggests shared pathophysiological pathways. POTS can contribute to fatigue and cognitive symptoms in fibromyalgia.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / Hypermobility spectrum disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and hypermobility spectrum disorder are significantly over-represented in fibromyalgia populations. Joint instability, repeated microtrauma, and the chronic pain burden of hypermobility may sensitise the central nervous system and precipitate or amplify fibromyalgia.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety disorders are significantly more prevalent in fibromyalgia than in the general population. The relationship is bidirectional: chronic pain amplifies anxiety, and anxiety amplifies pain perception. Both share serotonergic and noradrenergic dysregulation.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Major depression co-occurs with fibromyalgia at rates of 20–40%. The neurochemical pathways involved in mood regulation (serotonin, norepinephrine) are the same ones implicated in descending pain inhibition, explaining both the overlap and why SNRIs address both conditions simultaneously.

related_conditions.name: Trauma / PTSD
related_conditions.explanation: Adverse childhood experiences, physical trauma, and PTSD are strongly associated with fibromyalgia onset. Trauma dysregulates the HPA axis and autonomic nervous system in ways that can lower pain thresholds and promote central sensitisation. Addressing unresolved trauma is often necessary for meaningful symptom improvement.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Hypothyroidism produces fatigue, muscle pain, cognitive impairment, and cold intolerance that closely mimics fibromyalgia. Thyroid function testing is essential before a fibromyalgia diagnosis is confirmed. Some people carry a fibromyalgia label when undertreated hypothyroidism is the primary driver.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: MCAS and fibromyalgia co-occur more often than expected, particularly in the hypermobility-POTS-fibromyalgia cluster. Mast cell mediators can sensitise peripheral pain receptors and contribute to neuroinflammation, potentially driving or maintaining central sensitisation.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Women with endometriosis have elevated rates of fibromyalgia. Chronic pelvic pain from endometriosis may over time sensitise central pain pathways, leading to widespread pain that persists even when endometriosis lesions are treated. Both conditions share central sensitisation as a key mechanism.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Functional dyspepsia, like IBS, is considered part of the spectrum of disorders of gut-brain interaction and shares central sensitisation mechanisms with fibromyalgia. The co-occurrence of multiple functional somatic syndromes in one individual is well-documented.

related_conditions.name: ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: ADHD and fibromyalgia share noradrenergic dysregulation and overlap in cognitive symptoms. There is emerging evidence of elevated ADHD rates in fibromyalgia populations. The cognitive symptoms of fibromyalgia — inattention, working memory problems — can be mistaken for ADHD, and vice versa.

related_conditions.name: Chronic fatigue syndrome / ME/CFS
related_conditions.explanation: ME/CFS and fibromyalgia share fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and post-exertional symptom worsening, and many people meet criteria for both. The key distinguishing feature of ME/CFS is mandatory post-exertional malaise; fibromyalgia is more pain-dominated. Treatment approaches differ significantly, particularly around exercise.

related_conditions.name: Lupus (SLE)
related_conditions.explanation: Systemic lupus erythematosus can cause widespread pain, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms that closely mimic fibromyalgia. ANA testing is necessary to rule out SLE. Fibromyalgia can also occur alongside lupus, sometimes being misattributed to lupus flares.

related_conditions.name: Rheumatoid arthritis
related_conditions.explanation: Early or seronegative rheumatoid arthritis can present with widespread joint pain and morning stiffness similar to fibromyalgia. Inflammatory markers and joint imaging help distinguish them. Fibromyalgia can also occur concurrently in people with established RA.

related_conditions.name: Small fibre neuropathy
related_conditions.explanation: Small fibre neuropathy (SFN) produces burning pain, sensory hypersensitivity, and autonomic symptoms that can be indistinguishable from fibromyalgia clinically. Skin punch biopsy showing reduced intra-epidermal nerve fibre density can confirm SFN. A subset of people diagnosed with fibromyalgia may have unrecognised SFN.

related_conditions.name: Polymyalgia rheumatica
related_conditions.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica causes bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain with morning stiffness, typically in older adults, and can be confused with fibromyalgia. Unlike fibromyalgia, it is associated with significantly elevated ESR/CRP and responds dramatically to corticosteroids.

strategies.name: Graded aerobic exercise
strategies.description: Low-impact aerobic activity — walking, swimming, cycling, water aerobics — gradually increased in duration and intensity over weeks to months. Exercise is the single most evidence-supported intervention for fibromyalgia. It modulates central pain processing, improves sleep, reduces fatigue, and improves mood. The key is starting well below perceived capacity and increasing slowly to avoid triggering flares.
strategies.notes: Post-exertional flares are common when starting — beginning with as little as 5–10 minutes per day is appropriate for severely deconditioned individuals. High-intensity exercise is not recommended and can worsen symptoms. People with concurrent hypermobility or POTS need modified protocols.

strategies.name: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT) for pain
strategies.description: Pain-focused CBT addresses unhelpful thought patterns (catastrophising, hypervigilance to symptoms), activity avoidance behaviours, and sleep disruption. Multiple randomised controlled trials support modest but meaningful improvements in pain, function, and mood. Delivered by a psychologist trained in chronic pain or via validated online programmes.
strategies.notes: CBT does not imply pain is 'psychological' in a dismissive sense — it works by modifying neural pathways involved in pain amplification. Some people resist this framing. Pain neuroscience education delivered alongside CBT may improve engagement.

strategies.name: Pain neuroscience education (PNE)
strategies.description: Learning the mechanisms of central sensitisation — how the nervous system becomes dysregulated and amplifies pain — can itself reduce pain catastrophising and improve outcomes. Understanding that pain does not equal tissue damage changes the threatening nature of pain signals and can reduce their amplitude. Books, clinician-led sessions, and online programmes are available.
strategies.notes: PNE works best as part of a broader programme. It is most effective when delivered with empathy and validation rather than used to dismiss symptoms as 'all in the mind'.

strategies.name: Duloxetine (Cymbalta) — SNRI
strategies.description: An SNRI antidepressant that enhances norepinephrine and serotonin activity in descending pain inhibitory pathways. FDA-approved specifically for fibromyalgia. Reduces pain intensity by approximately 30% in responders and improves fatigue and mood. Takes 4–6 weeks to assess effectiveness.
strategies.notes: Not effective for everyone. Common side effects include nausea (often transient), insomnia, and sweating. Should be tapered rather than stopped abruptly. Prescribing requires a physician.

strategies.name: Pregabalin (Lyrica) or gabapentin
strategies.description: Anticonvulsants that reduce central nervous system hyperexcitability by modulating calcium channel activity. Pregabalin is FDA-approved for fibromyalgia. Can reduce pain and improve sleep quality in some individuals. May cause significant sedation, weight gain, and cognitive blunting.
strategies.notes: Side effect burden leads many people to discontinue. Weight gain is a common reason for stopping. Dependency and withdrawal effects are possible with prolonged use. Not suitable as a first-line choice for everyone.

strategies.name: Low-dose amitriptyline
strategies.description: A tricyclic antidepressant used at low doses (10–50mg nightly) specifically for its effects on sleep architecture and pain modulation rather than its antidepressant effect. One of the most commonly used medications for fibromyalgia in clinical practice. Can improve sleep quality and reduce morning stiffness.
strategies.notes: Not FDA-approved specifically for fibromyalgia but widely used off-label. Side effects include morning grogginess, dry mouth, and weight gain. Long-term evidence is less robust than for duloxetine or pregabalin.

strategies.name: Sleep hygiene and sleep disorder treatment
strategies.description: Addressing sleep quality is often one of the highest-leverage interventions in fibromyalgia. Poor sleep worsens pain, and pain worsens sleep — breaking this cycle through consistent sleep schedules, sleep restriction therapy, treating sleep apnoea if present, and reducing sleep disruptors (alcohol, irregular timing, screens) can produce meaningful improvements in pain and energy.
strategies.notes: If sleep apnoea is present, CPAP treatment can dramatically improve fibromyalgia symptoms. Restless legs syndrome, also common in fibromyalgia, responds to specific treatments and should be identified separately.

strategies.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
strategies.description: An 8-week structured programme combining mindfulness meditation, body scan practices, and gentle movement. Evidence supports moderate reductions in pain, fatigue, and psychological distress in fibromyalgia. Works by improving attentional control over pain signals and reducing stress-related nervous system activation.
strategies.notes: Requires consistent practice to maintain benefits. Body scan exercises may initially increase symptom awareness — this is normal. In-person programmes and validated apps (Insight Timer, Headspace clinical programmes) are available.

strategies.name: Low-impact strength and flexibility training
strategies.description: Gentle resistance training and stretching — yoga, tai chi, aquatic exercise — can improve muscle function, reduce stiffness, and support aerobic exercise capacity. Tai chi and yoga have specific evidence in fibromyalgia. Both are low-risk and can be adapted to any ability level.
strategies.notes: Yoga and tai chi may be better tolerated than conventional gym exercise initially. People with hypermobility should seek instruction from a teacher familiar with joint protection.

strategies.name: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN)
strategies.description: At doses of 1–5mg per day (far below addiction treatment doses), naltrexone may modulate microglial activity in the central nervous system, reducing neuroinflammation and pain amplification. Small trials and extensive patient-reported evidence suggest meaningful pain reduction for some individuals. Not currently approved for fibromyalgia.
strategies.notes: Used off-label — requires a physician willing to prescribe. Some compounding pharmacies can produce the low-dose formulation. Well-tolerated with minimal side effects at these doses. Larger trials are ongoing.

strategies.name: Anti-inflammatory dietary patterns
strategies.description: Observational data and patient reports suggest that diets high in processed foods, refined sugars, and seed oils may worsen fibromyalgia symptoms, while Mediterranean-style or whole-food patterns may reduce them. No single diet is established as standard of care, but reducing dietary inflammatory load is a reasonable low-risk strategy.
strategies.notes: A strict elimination diet approach is not supported by strong evidence. Moderation and sustainability matter more than strict rules. Food sensitivity responses are variable between individuals.

strategies.name: Vitamin D supplementation (if deficient)
strategies.description: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with musculoskeletal pain and fatigue, and is common in fibromyalgia populations. Correcting deficiency (25-OH vitamin D < 50 nmol/L) with supplementation has shown benefit in some trials for fibromyalgia symptoms. Testing before supplementing allows dosing to be targeted.
strategies.notes: Benefit is most likely in those who are genuinely deficient. Supplementation at high doses without monitoring can cause toxicity. Standard guidance is 1000–4000 IU daily for maintenance; therapeutic doses for deficiency should be guided by a clinician.

strategies.name: Pacing and energy management
strategies.description: Deliberately distributing activity throughout the day and building in rest before reaching exhaustion — rather than the boom-bust cycle of overdoing it on good days and crashing on bad days. Pacing reduces peak symptoms, helps identify sustainable activity levels, and builds tolerance gradually over time.
strategies.notes: Differs from the graded exercise therapy used in ME/CFS — the goal in fibromyalgia is to find a sustainable baseline and expand it slowly, not complete rest. A physiotherapist or pain psychologist can help establish an individualised pacing plan.

things_to_avoid: High-intensity exercise when first starting — it commonly triggers severe flares and can entrench the pain cycle rather than interrupt it
Complete rest and deconditioning — prolonged inactivity worsens central sensitisation and increases pain sensitivity over time

Opioid analgesics for fibromyalgia management — evidence does not support their effectiveness in central sensitisation pain and they carry risks of opioid-induced hyperalgesia, worsening the underlying mechanism
The boom-bust activity pattern — pushing through on good days until exhausted, then being unable to function for several days, is one of the most common patterns that prevents improvement
Excessive focus on catastrophising or worst-case interpretations of symptoms — not a character failing, but a neurologically-mediated tendency that genuinely amplifies pain and can be modified

Dismissing or ignoring co-occurring conditions — sleep apnoea, thyroid dysfunction, vitamin D deficiency, and POTS can each independently worsen fibromyalgia symptoms and are worth identifying and treating
Stopping medications abruptly — particularly duloxetine, pregabalin, and amitriptyline require gradual tapering to avoid withdrawal effects

Pursuing repeated surgical or invasive procedures for pain that has no structural correlate — central sensitisation pain does not respond to structural interventions and surgery can itself worsen sensitisation

uncomfortable_truths.truth: There is no cure, and the goal is not to find the one treatment that makes fibromyalgia go away — it is to build a life that works despite it.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: A diagnosis without a clear cause feels like an incomplete explanation. Many people continue searching for a missed diagnosis, a food trigger, or a supplement that will resolve everything. This search can last years and delays engagement with the approaches that actually help.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who make the most sustained progress are those who stop waiting for a cure and begin building a consistent, multi-pronged programme — sleep management, paced movement, psychological skills, and social support. This is not resignation; it is the most effective strategy.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Exercise is one of the most effective treatments for fibromyalgia, and avoiding it because it causes pain is one of the main reasons people stay severely affected.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Post-exertional pain flares are real and discouraging. The rational response to something that causes pain is to stop doing it. The paradox of fibromyalgia is that controlled movement gradually downregulates the sensitised pain system, while rest entrains it further.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Starting with 5 minutes of gentle walking and increasing by 1–2 minutes per week, without exceeding the point of significant flare, is a concrete entry point. Many people who could barely walk for 10 minutes eventually build to 30–45 minutes daily over 6–12 months.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Trauma and psychological history are not just comorbidities to be treated separately — they are often part of the mechanism that maintains the pain, and addressing them is part of treating fibromyalgia itself.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Being told psychological factors are involved can feel like being told the pain isn't real, or that it's a personal failing. This conflation leads many people to reject any psychological framing, even evidence-based approaches that directly address central nervous system dysregulation.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Trauma-informed therapy approaches (EMDR, somatic therapy, ACT) work on the same nervous system dysregulation pathways that maintain fibromyalgia pain. People who engage with these approaches often report improvements in pain that medication did not produce.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many mainstream clinicians do not understand fibromyalgia well, and receiving poor care — dismissal, over-medicalisation, or a prescription alone without other support — is common.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients trust that doctors have answers and that following medical advice should lead to improvement. Accepting that the standard of care is often inadequate requires taking greater personal responsibility for one's own care navigation, which is exhausting.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who fare best typically build a small team — a rheumatologist or pain specialist who takes the condition seriously, a physiotherapist experienced in fibromyalgia, and a psychologist familiar with chronic pain — rather than relying on a single GP. This requires advocacy but is achievable.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Fibromyalgia amplifies all physical sensations, which means that normal age-related changes, benign symptoms, and bodily noise get interpreted as alarming — leading to excessive investigations and a spiral of medical anxiety.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: When the nervous system is sensitised, symptoms feel threatening and urgent. It is genuinely difficult to distinguish amplified normal sensation from a new pathological signal when everything feels more intense than it should.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Working with a pain psychologist to develop a reliable framework for evaluating symptoms — distinguishing fibromyalgia flare patterns from genuinely new concerns — reduces unnecessary investigations and reduces the anxiety that worsens pain.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Exercise is the most robustly evidenced intervention for fibromyalgia. It directly downregulates central sensitisation, improves sleep architecture, releases endogenous opioids and endocannabinoids, and reduces psychological distress. Without movement, almost all other interventions are less effective.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin with 5–10 minutes of walking, cycling, or swimming at a comfortable pace. Maintain this for two weeks without increasing. If no significant flare results, add 2–3 minutes. Continue this pattern over 3–6 months.
key_levers.timeline: 3–6 months to establish a meaningful habit; 6–12 months to see significant improvement in pain and function

key_levers.why_it_matters: Non-restorative sleep is not a secondary symptom — it actively maintains central sensitisation. Improving sleep quality breaks one of the core positive feedback loops driving fibromyalgia. Sleep disorders (apnoea, restless legs) also need to be identified and treated.
key_levers.how_to_start: Establish a consistent sleep-wake time 7 days a week. Reduce alcohol, which disrupts sleep architecture. Assess for sleep apnoea if there is snoring, witnessed apnoeas, or severe daytime fatigue. Discuss low-dose amitriptyline with a doctor if sleep is severely disrupted.
key_levers.timeline: 2–6 weeks for sleep hygiene changes to show effect; longer for medication titration

key_levers.lever: Pain neuroscience education combined with psychological skills
key_levers.why_it_matters: Understanding that pain is a nervous system output — not necessarily a signal of ongoing tissue damage — reduces pain catastrophising, which is one of the strongest predictors of disability in fibromyalgia. CBT, ACT, and MBSR all produce measurable neural changes in pain processing when practised consistently.
key_levers.how_to_start: Read 'Explain Pain' by Butler and Moseley. Access a psychologist trained in chronic pain or ACT. Consider an 8-week MBSR course (online options available).
key_levers.timeline: 4–8 weeks to see shifts in pain catastrophising scores; 3–6 months for significant functional changes

key_levers.why_it_matters: Undiagnosed sleep apnoea, hypothyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, small fibre neuropathy, POTS, and MCAS can each independently amplify fibromyalgia symptoms. Treating any of these can produce disproportionate improvements in the overall symptom burden.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a comprehensive blood panel including thyroid function, vitamin D, ferritin, and B12. Discuss sleep study referral if sleep apnoea is plausible. Ask a rheumatologist to assess for small fibre neuropathy if burning pain is prominent.
key_levers.timeline: Weeks to months depending on what is found and treated

key_levers.why_it_matters: The boom-bust pattern (overdoing on good days, crashing on bad days) is one of the most common and damaging patterns in fibromyalgia. It prevents the establishment of a baseline from which gradual improvement can be built, and produces repeated nervous system flares that deepen sensitisation.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify the activity level that can be sustained without triggering a flare. Make this the daily baseline, including on good days. Resist the urge to do more on good days. Use a brief symptom log to track patterns.
key_levers.timeline: Several weeks to establish a sustainable baseline; improvement from that baseline over months

key_levers.why_it_matters: Fibromyalgia is poorly served by single-clinician management. A rheumatologist or pain specialist, a physiotherapist experienced in chronic pain, and a psychologist trained in pain management form the minimum effective team. Each covers different parts of the condition that no single practitioner adequately manages alone.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask the current GP for referral to a specialist pain clinic or rheumatologist. Search specifically for physiotherapists with chronic pain experience. Look for psychologists listing 'ACT for chronic pain' or 'CBT for chronic pain' as a specialty.
key_levers.timeline: Months to assemble and establish effective working relationships with a team

common_mistakes: Starting exercise at too high an intensity and experiencing severe flares, then concluding that exercise is harmful — this entrenches avoidance and prevents improvement

Relying on medication alone without incorporating movement, sleep management, and psychological approaches — medications provide partial benefit and do not address the underlying sensitisation mechanism
Seeking a definitive cause or missed diagnosis for years rather than beginning evidence-based management — delays measurable improvement
Pushing through fatigue and pain without pacing, then crashing — the boom-bust cycle prevents establishing a sustainable baseline

Discontinuing medications or programmes after a few weeks without giving adequate time to assess effectiveness — most effective interventions for fibromyalgia take 6–12 weeks to show meaningful results
Interpreting every new or changed symptom as a sign of a dangerous new disease, leading to repeated investigations and increasing medical anxiety
Avoiding all psychological support because it implies the pain is imagined — this forfeits access to some of the most effective treatments available
Relying heavily on opioid analgesics, which are ineffective for central sensitisation pain and can worsen it through opioid-induced hyperalgesia over time

specialists.role: Typically the specialist who makes or confirms the fibromyalgia diagnosis after ruling out inflammatory, autoimmune, and connective tissue conditions. Can also assess for co-occurring conditions such as hypermobility, Sjögren's syndrome, or lupus.
specialists.notes: Quality of care varies significantly. Some rheumatologists diagnose fibromyalgia and discharge without further support; others actively manage it. Look for one who views fibromyalgia as a legitimate neurological condition rather than a diagnosis of exclusion with no further management.

specialists.role: Specialist pain clinics offer the most comprehensive management: medication optimisation, physiotherapy, pain psychology, and education programmes. Multidisciplinary pain programmes have the strongest evidence base for improving function and quality of life in fibromyalgia.
specialists.notes: Waiting lists for specialist pain clinics can be long in public health systems. Some private pain clinics offer faster access. Pain programmes that include psychological and physiotherapy components outperform medication-only approaches.

specialists.role: Designs individualised, graded movement programmes that begin at a manageable level and build gradually. Teaches pain neuroscience, pacing strategies, and body mechanics. Particularly important for safely initiating exercise in people who have been severely limited.
specialists.notes: Not all physiotherapists are experienced with fibromyalgia — look for those who mention chronic pain, central sensitisation, or graded activity in their profiles. Physiotherapists trained in Explain Pain or ACT approaches are particularly useful.

specialists.type: Psychologist trained in chronic pain (CBT or ACT)
specialists.role: Addresses pain catastrophising, activity avoidance, sleep disruption, and the psychological impact of chronic illness. Pain-focused CBT and ACT are among the most evidence-supported interventions for fibromyalgia.
specialists.notes: General counsellors or therapists without chronic pain training are less effective. Look specifically for those listing chronic pain, health psychology, or ACT for pain as specialties. Some pain clinics have embedded psychologists.

specialists.role: Diagnoses and treats sleep disorders that co-occur with and worsen fibromyalgia — particularly sleep apnoea, restless legs syndrome, and periodic limb movement disorder. Treating these can produce significant improvements in pain and fatigue.
specialists.notes: Particularly indicated when fatigue is disproportionately severe, when snoring or witnessed apnoeas are reported, or when standard fibromyalgia treatments have had limited effect.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative physician
specialists.role: Investigates potential contributing factors that mainstream rheumatology may not address: vitamin and mineral deficiencies, hormonal factors, gut health, mould exposure, and small fibre neuropathy. Some people find this approach valuable when standard care has not produced improvement.
specialists.notes: Quality varies significantly. Look for practitioners with formal medical training who apply evidence-based investigation rather than extensive supplement protocols. Useful for identifying undertreated co-occurring conditions.

specialists.role: Relevant when small fibre neuropathy is suspected (burning pain, prominent sensory symptoms) — skin punch biopsy is a neurological investigation. Also relevant for headache management and for ruling out other neurological conditions.
specialists.notes: Not routinely required but should be considered when burning or electric-shock pain is prominent, or when standard fibromyalgia diagnosis is uncertain.

patient_communities.name: r/Fibromyalgia
patient_communities.notes: Large active community for shared experience, practical tips, medication experiences, and emotional support. A useful space for people who have recently been diagnosed and want to hear from others living with the condition long-term.

patient_communities.name: Fibromyalgia Action UK (FMA UK)
patient_communities.notes: UK-based charity offering helplines, local support groups, and patient information. Useful for UK patients navigating NHS referrals and accessing support.

patient_communities.name: National Fibromyalgia Association (NFA)
patient_communities.notes: US-based patient advocacy and education organisation. Provides clinician finder tools, patient education materials, and research updates.

patient_communities.name: Fibromyalgia & Chronic Pain Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Multiple large Facebook groups exist for fibromyalgia. Quality varies — look for groups with active moderation and a focus on evidence-based approaches alongside peer support.

patient_communities.name: TalkFibro
patient_communities.notes: Dedicated fibromyalgia forum with threads covering symptoms, medications, research, and daily life management. Tends to attract people who have been living with fibromyalgia for longer and have developed detailed practical knowledge.

learning_resources.title: Explain Pain
learning_resources.description: The foundational text on pain neuroscience education, written for patients. Explains in accessible language how the nervous system produces pain, why central sensitisation occurs, and how understanding these mechanisms can itself reduce pain. One of the most widely recommended books in chronic pain management.

learning_resources.title: The Fibromyalgia Solution
learning_resources.description: A clinician-authored practical guide to fibromyalgia management covering medication, exercise, sleep, and psychological approaches. Useful for people newly diagnosed who want a comprehensive overview of what is known and what helps.

learning_resources.title: Fibromyalgia — Clinical Practice Guidelines
learning_resources.author_or_source: Canadian Pain Society / European League Against Rheumatism (EULAR)
learning_resources.description: The EULAR 2017 evidence-based recommendations for fibromyalgia management provide a rigorous summary of what the evidence supports. Useful for people who want to understand what interventions have formal clinical backing and compare what their clinicians are offering.

learning_resources.title: Fibromyalgia Network
learning_resources.description: US-based patient education organisation with summaries of fibromyalgia research, treatment updates, and articles on living with the condition. Accessible language with a focus on keeping patients current on research developments.

learning_resources.title: Why Does It Still Hurt?
learning_resources.description: An accessible and compassionate exploration of chronic pain science for patients, covering central sensitisation, the nervous system's role in maintaining pain, and practical approaches to recovery. Bridges the gap between research and everyday understanding effectively.

learning_resources.title: The Pain Reprocessing Therapy approach — research overview
learning_resources.author_or_source: Yoni Ashar et al., JAMA Psychiatry, 2022
learning_resources.description: Landmark randomised controlled trial of Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) showing that a psychological intervention targeting the belief that pain signals tissue damage produced dramatic pain reductions. Relevant because fibromyalgia shares mechanisms with the central sensitisation pain studied here. Provides compelling evidence that the nervous system is the treatment target.

learning_resources.title: Fibromyalgia — Mayo Clinic Patient Overview
learning_resources.description: A well-written, accurate patient-facing summary from a major academic medical centre. Useful as a starting point or to share with family members who want a credible mainstream source on what fibromyalgia is.

learning_resources.title: Lorimer Moseley lectures on pain science
learning_resources.description: Professor Lorimer Moseley is one of the world's leading pain scientists. His lectures (particularly 'Why Things Hurt' on YouTube) explain central sensitisation, pain neuroscience, and the evidence base for pain education in an engaging, often humorous way. One of the best free introductions to modern pain science available.

learning_resources.title: Unlearn Your Pain
learning_resources.description: A structured programme based on the concept that central sensitisation and mind-body pain can be addressed through a combination of education, journalling, and psychological techniques. Some people with fibromyalgia find this approach transformative; others find it less applicable to their case. Worth engaging with critically.

sections.relevance_signal: **Widespread tender muscles and joints**, unrefreshing sleep, and brain fog alongside the gut symptoms.
